Summary: GATA1 is identified as the master erythroid transcription factor in hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs). The canonical inflammasome mediates the cleavage of GATA1 in hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs); erythroid differentiation triggers ribosomal stress–induced activation of the ZAKα/P38 axis, which phosphorylates and promotes NLRP1 assembly. Genetic inhibition of Nlrp1 increased erythrocyte counts in zebrafish larvae, and inhibition of ZAKα with nilotinib promoted erythroid differentiation and GATA1 accumulation in K562 cells.
Reference: Rodríguez-Ruiz, L., Lozano-Gil, J. M., Naranjo-Sánchez, E., Martínez-Balsalobre, E., Martínez-López, A., Lachaud, C., Blanquer, M., Phung, T. K., García-Moreno, D., Cayuela, M. L., Tyrkalska, S. D., Pérez-Oliva, A. B., & Mulero, V. (2023). ZAKα/P38 kinase signaling pathway regulates hematopoiesis by activating the NLRP1 inflammasome. EMBO Mol Med, 15(10), e18142. https://doi.org/10.15252/emmm.202318142

Summary: GATA1 is listed as a transcription factor that functions as a modulator of autophagy and thereby is among the modulators that regulate erythroid differentiation (the process generating mature red blood cells).
Reference: Zhang, J., Wu, K., Xiao, X., Liao, J., Hu, Q., Chen, H., Liu, J., & An, X. (2015). Autophagy as a regulatory component of erythropoiesis. Int J Mol Sci, 16(2), 4083-4094. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms16024083

Note: No relevant passage was found in this paper.

Summary: GATA1 plays a central role in the development of erythrocytes, especially terminal erythroid differentiation, and it is required for erythropoiesis from hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells. GATA1 is part of the core transcription factor network that regulates erythroid-specific gene expression networks. GATA1 is important, in a dose-dependent manner, for the survival of erythroid progenitors associated with erythropoietin (EPO) signaling. GATA1 is important, in a dose-dependent manner, for the cell-cycle regulation of erythroid progenitors associated with erythropoietin (EPO) signaling. GATA1 deficiency arrests erythropoiesis at the proerythroblast stage and induces apoptosis. FAM122A directly interacts with the C‑terminal zinc finger domain of GATA1 and reduces GATA1 chromatin occupancy on promoters of its target genes. The interaction results in decreased GATA1 transcriptional activity. Knockdown of GATA1, but not GATA2, significantly rescued the FAM122A-silencing–enhanced erythroid gene expression and maturation potential, indicating GATA1 mediates FAM122A-regulated erythroid differentiation. Abnormal regulation of GATA1 is associated with dyserythropoietic disorders.
Reference: Chen, J., Zhou, Q., Liu, M. H., Yang, Y. S., Wang, Y. Q., Huang, Y., & Chen, G. Q. (2020). FAM122A Inhibits Erythroid Differentiation through GATA1. Stem Cell Reports, 15(3), 721-734. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.stemcr.2020.07.010

Summary: Tg(gata1:dsRed) zebrafish embryos show specific dsRed fluorescence in circulating red blood cells; 75% of embryos were expected to be fluorescent.
Reference: Ding, Y., & Liu, F. (2022). Protocol for isolation and ATAC-seq library construction of zebrafish red blood cells. STAR Protoc, 3(4), 101889. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xpro.2022.101889
